article_title: Why We Chose the Harder Path: Docker Hardened Images, One Year Later
core_thesis: Docker Hardened Images (DHI) have reached over 500k daily pulls and 25k continuously patched OS artifacts one year after launch, demonstrating that a multi-distro, open-source, from-source patching approach with extensive signed attestations provides superior security and adoption compared to proprietary 'distroless' alternatives. Docker argues that security should not be a premium feature and that vendor lock-in through proprietary OSes undermines real-world security gains.
detailed_summary:
The article, written by Christian Dupuis, Senior Principal Software Engineer at Docker, reflects on the first year of Docker Hardened Images (DHI). Key milestones include crossing 500k daily pulls, 25k continuously patched OS-level artifacts, and a catalog of 2,000+ hardened images, MCP servers, Helm charts, and ELS images. Docker emphasizes that they chose the 'harder path' by making DHI free and open source (Apache 2.0), supporting multiple distributions (Debian and Alpine) rather than a proprietary OS, building every system package from source in a SLSA Build Level 3 pipeline, and shipping 17 signed attestations per image for independent verifiability.

The article contrasts Docker's approach with other vendors who create proprietary 'distroless' distributions, arguing that such distributions impose a migration tax, require retraining, and create vendor lock-in. Docker's multi-distro approach allows teams to stay on distros they already trust, reducing adoption time from quarters to hours. Docker also highlights their upstream commitment, such as creating a patch for CVE-2025-12735 (a 9.8 CRITICAL RCE in Kibana's dependency chain) when the upstream library was unmaintained. They advise customers to ask vendors about patching timelines, SBOM completeness (including compiled dependencies), advisory feed coverage, and use of VEX for exploitability justification. Docker also offers Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) for up to five years past end-of-life and automatically rebuilds customized images when CVEs are patched.

entities.name: Christian Dupuis
entities.role: Senior Principal Software Engineer at Docker and author of the article

entities.name: Docker Hardened Images (DHI)
entities.role: Docker's product for secure, minimal container images with multi-distro support and extensive attestations

entities.name: SLSA Build Level 3
entities.role: Supply-chain Levels for Software Artifacts framework; Docker's pipeline achieves Level 3 for cryptographic provenance

entities.name: CVE-2025-12735
entities.role: A 9.8 CRITICAL RCE vulnerability in Kibana's dependency chain that Docker patched and contributed upstream

entities.name: Attentive
entities.role: Customer that rolled out DHI across 200 services with 'zero drama'

entities.name: Debian
entities.role: One of the Linux distributions supported by DHI (alongside Alpine)

entities.name: Alpine
entities.role: One of the Linux distributions supported by DHI (alongside Debian)

entities.name: VEX
entities.role: Vulnerability Exploitability eXchange, a CISA-backed standard for communicating exploitability

key_data_points: Docker Hardened Images crossed 500k daily pulls and 25k continuously patched OS-level artifacts one year after launch (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: high adoption means more targets using DHI, but also a more secure baseline).

Docker builds every system package from source in a SLSA Build Level 3 pipeline with cryptographic provenance, ensuring traceability and integrity (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: supply chain attacks are harder; you'd need to compromise the build pipeline itself).

DHI ships 17 signed attestations per image, including CycloneDX and SPDX SBOMs, SLSA provenance, VEX, FIPS compliance, STIG scan results, secrets scan, virus scan, and changelog (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: these attestations provide a wealth of information for reconnaissance if you can access them).

Docker created a patch for CVE-2025-12735 (9.8 CRITICAL RCE in Kibana's dependency chain) when the upstream library was unmaintained, and contributed it to LangChain.js (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: demonstrates Docker's proactive security; but also shows that even critical CVEs can be missed by other vendors).

Docker's SBOMs include compiled dependencies (Rust crates, Go modules, JavaScript packages), unlike some vendors whose SBOMs only cover system-level packages (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: if you're scanning for vulnerabilities, you need complete SBOMs to find all attack surfaces).

Docker uses VEX to provide per-CVE exploitability justifications, rather than hiding CVEs by setting non-matching version ranges (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: VEX can be used to identify which CVEs are considered unexploitable, potentially revealing blind spots).

Docker automatically rebuilds customized images when a CVE is patched upstream, extending the security guarantee to user modifications (🟢 relevant to your DevOps: this reduces maintenance burden for custom images).

Docker offers Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) for up to five years past end-of-life, delivering security patches for unsupported software (🟢 relevant to your Homelab: useful for keeping legacy systems secure while planning upgrades).
methodology_steps: To adopt Docker Hardened Images: 1) Explore the DHI catalog at https://www.docker.com/products/hardened-images/. 2) Choose between Alpine or Debian-based images (both amd64 and arm64). 3) Replace your existing base image with the corresponding DHI image in your Dockerfile. 4) Optionally customize the image by adding your own packages; Docker will automatically rebuild and patch your custom image when CVEs are fixed. 5) Verify the image's attestations using Docker Scout or third-party tools like Grype, Trivy, Wiz, or Mend. 6) For enterprise features, sign up for a paid tier; for individual use, the free DHI Community tier is available under Apache 2.0 license.
conclusions: Docker's multi-distro, open-source approach to hardened images reduces adoption friction and vendor lock-in, making it easier for teams to improve their security posture without migrating to a proprietary OS. This is supported by customer testimony (Attentive: '200 services – zero drama').

Docker's investment in from-source patching, complete SBOMs, and signed attestations provides a higher level of supply chain security than vendors who only optimize for clean CVE scans. The article provides concrete examples of gaps in other vendors' advisory feeds and SBOM completeness.

For Red Teaming, the extensive attestations and transparency of DHI can be a double-edged sword: they provide defenders with verifiable evidence, but also give attackers detailed information about the image's contents and build process if accessed.
For DevOps and Homelab users, DHI's automatic rebuilding of customized images and ELS support reduce maintenance overhead and allow secure operation of legacy software.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in cybersecurity (Red Teaming), DevOps, and homelabbing. For Red Teaming, understanding Docker's hardened image pipeline reveals both defensive strengths (from-source patching, signed attestations, VEX) and potential attack surfaces (SBOMs and attestations can be used for reconnaissance). For DevOps, the multi-distro support and automatic patching of custom images directly reduce operational burden. For homelabbing, the free DHI Community tier and ELS support allow you to run secure containers on familiar distros without cost. The article also provides concrete questions to ask vendors, which you can apply when evaluating any container security solution.
extra_advice: Consider setting up a homelab environment using DHI Community images to experiment with their attestations and SBOMs. Use tools like Grype or Trivy to scan the images and compare the results with Docker's own VEX data. This hands-on experience will deepen your understanding of supply chain security and give you practical skills for both Red Teaming (identifying gaps) and DevOps (securing your own pipelines).
